coredata.dc:title: Novel and Reliable Chemosensor Based on C. dots from Sunflower seeds for the Distinct Detection of Picric Acid and Bilirubin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Roshni, V.
Gujar, Varsha
Muntjeeb, Syed
Doshi, Pooja
Ottoor, Divya
coredata.dc:description: Based on the green chemistry approach, highly fluorescent and novel carbon dots (C. dots) were synthesized from naturally available and cost effective sunflower seeds. The obtained C. dots showed a fluorescence quantum yield (Q.Y) of 9.5% with high water dispersibility and photostability. The obtained C. dots were employed for the detection of picric acid (PA) and bilirubin. A good linear relationship in the range of 20–60 nM was obtained for PA with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 3.86 nM. C. dots were successfully incorporated in the agarose matrix which enabled them to be employed as a solid platform for the in situ detection of PA. The fluorescence of C. dots was selectively quenched by bilirubin compared to other biomolecules with a LOD of 2.03 μM. Use of C. dots as potential candidate for bilirubin detection was verified by real sample analysis. Further, the separation of C. dots was performed using column chromatography and the optical properties of the two different fractions obtained were studied. The blue fraction of C. dots was found to exhibit a higher fluorescence Q.Y and excitation independent emission, with an improved detection of PA and bilirubin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C. dots
Sunflower seeds
Picric acid
Bilirubin
Hydrothermal synthesis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing of PA and bilirubin
Preparation of standard solutions for real sample assay
Collection of real sample and detection of bilirubin
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of C. dots by PA
Practical application of C. dots as chemo sensor
Selective and sensitive detection of bilirubin
Detection of bilirubin in real sample
Characterization of fractions of C. dots obtained by column chromatography
Detection of PA using blue C. dots
Improved detection of bilirubin using C. dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumental Facility (SAIF) and Central Surface Analytical Facility of IIT Bombay for HRTEM and XPS facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (B) HRTEM image and (C) particle size distribution of C. dots produced by the hydrothermal carbonization of sunflower seeds.
FTIR spectrum of the synthesized C. dots.
(A) XPS full scan spectrum of C. dots. XPS high resolution spectra of (B) C 1s (C) O 1s (D) N 1s of C. dots synthesized from sunflower seeds.
(A) UV–Vis. absorption spectra of C. dots and (B) Fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelength ranging from 290 nm to 430 nm. Inset: Photographs of C. dots under normal and UV light.
(A) Fluorescence quenching response of different aromatic compounds towards C. dots (B) Variation of fluorescence intensity of C. dots with different concentration of PA ranging from 0.1 to 1 µM.
Stern-Volmer linear plot (S.V plot) showing the relationship between F0/F with the range of concentration of PA from 20 nM to 60 nM.
(A) Overlapping of absorbance spectrum of PA with the excitation and emission spectrum of C. dots showing IFE (B) Comparison of absorbance spectrum of other interfering aromatics with PA.
(i) Agarose gel disc (ii) C. dots in agarose gel and (iii) C. dots-PA in agarose gel disc A) under normal light B) under UV light.
(A) Quenching of fluorescence of C. dots with bilirubin addition which shows the sensitivity of C. dots for bilirubin detection (B) S.V plot showing linear correlation.
Calibration plot of (F0-F)/F0 at λex of 360 nm for standard solution of bilirubin.
(A) The absorption spectra of the fractions of C. dots (B) and (C) shows the fluorescence spectra of blue and green fractions respectively.
(A) Quenching of C. dots fluorescence with increase in concentration of PA from 1 nM to 10 nM and (B) S.V plot.
(A) Quenching of C. dots with increase in concentration of bilirubin from 1 µM to 10 μM and (B) S.V plot with LOD 0.27 µM.
Comparison of PA detection using C. dots synthesised using various precursors.
Addition of bilirubin to urine mimic sample.

head.ce:title: Novel and Reliable Chemosensor Based on C. dots from Sunflower seeds for the Distinct Detection of Picric Acid and Bilirubin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Savitribai Phule Pune University, Pune, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Savitribai Phule Pune University, Pune, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the green chemistry approach, highly fluorescent and novel carbon dots (C. dots) were synthesized from naturally available and cost effective sunflower seeds. The obtained C. dots showed a fluorescence quantum yield (Q.Y) of 9.5% with high water dispersibility and photostability. The obtained C. dots were employed for the detection of picric acid (PA) and bilirubin. A good linear relationship in the range of 20–60 nM was obtained for PA with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 3.86 nM. C. dots were successfully incorporated in the agarose matrix which enabled them to be employed as a solid platform for the in situ detection of PA. The fluorescence of C. dots was selectively quenched by bilirubin compared to other biomolecules with a LOD of 2.03 μM. Use of C. dots as potential candidate for bilirubin detection was verified by real sample analysis. Further, the separation of C. dots was performed using column chromatography and the optical properties of the two different fractions obtained were studied. The blue fraction of C. dots was found to exhibit a higher fluorescence Q.Y and excitation independent emission, with an improved detection of PA and bilirubin.

ce:section.ce:para: Picric acid (2,4,6- trinitrophenol, PA) is an inevitable member of nitro aromatics due to its explosive nature and industrial applications. Owing to the presence of three nitro groups, PA is considered as the most acidic phenol. It is mainly used as military explosive and was the most popular explosive extensively used in bombs and grenades during World War I [1]. PA has more intense explosive ability than TNT, another well known member of the nitro aromatic family [2]. Apart from being used as an explosive, it also finds application as a dye (yellow dye in dye industry), pesticide and antiseptic [3]. Due to its high water solubility, a small amount of PA can cause environmental pollution. In humans, PA causes severe poisoning and the ‘Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health concentration’ (IDLH) value for PA is 75 mg/m3 [4]. Among the various spectroscopic and electrochemical techniques for detection of PA, fluorescence spectroscopy is highly appreciated due to its simplicity and high sensitivity. Use of metal organic frameworks, semiconductor Q. dots, nanoclusters, etc. are identified as good fluorophores for PA detection [5–7]. Main limitation for the applications of such system is the lack of good sensitivity or the involvement of organic solvents and poisonous metals. This, points towards the importance of rapid and accurate determination of PA by simple and cost effective analytical techniques.

Bilirubin, a potentially toxic substance produced in the bone marrow cells is an end product of breakdown of red blood cells [8]. Liver metabolises bilirubin and usually excreted from the body via bile. The normal range of bilirubin in blood serum is 0.2–1.2 mg/dL for adults and 5–5.2 mg/dL for children [9]. Any type of accumulation of bilirubin above this normal range causes hyperbilirubinemia and jaundice in which a yellow tinge of skin, eyes and body fluids is observed [10,11]. In new-borns, the increased level of bilirubin causes hearing loss and brain damage and eventually leads to Kernicterus which is caused by bilirubin neuro toxicity [12]. Jaundice in adults results in inflamed liver or obstructed bile duct due to rapid break down or destruction of red blood cells [13]. The highly recommended analytical methods for the detection of bilirubin includes diazo methods, peroxidise method, fluorometric methods etc. [14].

C. dots being the smallest and youngest member of carbon nanomaterial family, gained remarkable attention due to its splendid physical and optical properties. Adhering to the fundamental principles of sustainable development, they are synthesised from safe, easily available, cheap and naturally occurring precursors via green synthetic methodology [15–17]. The attributes like green synthesis which is free of any chemical reagents like acids, bases or salts, good aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, etc. enable C. dots to be used as an excellent sensing probe for various environmentally hazardous metal ions like Hg2+, Cr6+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, etc. by fluorescence quenching of C. dots [18–20]. In the biological front it has been used for the detection of biomolecules [21–23]. Selective and sensitive detection of PA using C. dots has been reported previously by some research groups. Zhang et al. has used C. dots synthesised from edetic acid and acrylamide for the detection of PA with a limit of detection of 0.046 μM [24]. Conducting polypyrrole-C. dots nano composite was used by Chattopadhyay et al. for the detection of PA [25]. As the modern scientific community is more inclined towards the green chemistry, the detection of PA using natural precursors is of increased importance. Even though various analytical techniques are available for the detection of bilirubin, the detection using C. dots through IFE was not reported so far.

In recent years, inner filter effect (IFE) which was earlier considered as an error in spectrometric analysis, has transformed into an effective sensing tool [26]. IFE results from the spectral overlap between the absorption band of the analyte in the detection system and exciting or emitting band of fluorophore. IFE is considered as a simpler and flexible mode of interaction in fluorometry as it does not require the chemical linkage between analyte and fluorophore. However, other photophysical processes require the actual contact between the fluorophore and the quencher. Application of IFE using carbon dots (C. dots) as a fluorophore has been implemented by various researchers for the sensitive detection of several kinds of environmentally hazardous and biologically active molecules [26].

For further understanding of the structural properties and scrutinizing the application of C. dots in bioimaging and biosensing, especially where high fluorescence quantum yield (Q.Y) is essential, separation and purification of C. dots can be undertaken. One of the separation techniques used so far for the C. dot analysis was polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE). C. dots obtained from candle soot was separated using PAGE and three different fractions were obtained [27]. Capillary electrophoresis was another method used for the separation of C. dots [28]. Owing to the easy and bulk collection of the separated fractions, chromatographic separation techniques are also much preferred [29].

In this work, C. dots have been synthesised from sunflower seeds obtained from oilseed sunflower crops mainly grown for the production of sunflower oil. The seeds have rich oil content which is the main source of poly unsaturated oil. They also contain vitamins, protein and trace elements like phosphorus, manganese, magnesium, etc. One pot hydrothermal method has been employed for the synthesis of C. dots. The highly fluorescent C. dots were then effectively applied for the detection of PA and bilirubin.

Sunflower seeds were collected from the local grocery shop. These were washed, dried and powdered. Bilirubin and haemoglobin were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Aromatic compounds like PA, ammonium nitrate (AN), benzoic acid (BA), 3,5- dinitrobenzoic acid (DNB), phenol, p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and p-nitrotoluene (p-NT) were supplied by HiMedia Laboratories, Mumbai. The solutions of the metal ions (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CoSO4·7H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Fe2(SO4)3, MgSO4·7H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O and NiSO4·7H2O) were prepared from their respective salts purchased from SDFCL. Amino acids and other biomolecules were procured from HiMedia Laboratories, Mumbai. Dialysis bags (molecular wt. Cut off-1000 Da) were supplied by Sigma Aldrich.

The size and microstructure of C. dots from sunflower seeds were obtained by high resolution transmission electron microscope, HRTEM (TECNAI G2, F30, Make: FEI) with an accelerating potential of 300 kV. Scanning Electron Microscope, SEM (Model: Nova Nanosem 450) was used for finding the surface morphology of the obtained C. dots. The absorbance measurements were recorded using a UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1800 spectrophotometer) by scanning the spectrum from wavelength ranging from 200 to 600 nm. The fluorescence spectra of samples were obtained using Jasco FP-8300 steady state spectrofluorometer with 150 W Xe lamp. The steady state fluorescence measurements were taken with an excitation and emission slit width of 2.5 nm and a scan speed of 1000 nm/min. FTIR-8400, Shimadzu Infrared spectrometer, was used to characterize the functional groups on the C. dots by scanning the spectra from 4000 to 500 cm−1. Fluorescence lifetime measurements of the samples were carried out using Time Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC-Make: Horiba Jobin Yuvon Fluorocube-01-NL Fluorescence Life time System). XPS analysis of the C. dots were performed on AXIS Supra instrument (Make: Kratos Analytical UK) - Shimadzu group with Monochromatic (AlKα) 600 W X-ray source (1486.6 eV) as the source for excitation.

C. dots were synthesised via hydrothermal method. 1 g of powdered sunflower seeds was taken with 30 mL of water in a 50 mL teflon lined autoclave and kept in the furnace for 6 h. at 250 °C. The obtained dark brownish yellow oily liquid containing black residue was centrifuged and filtered to remove oil and bigger sized particles. The filtrate was then dialysed for 24 h. The resulted solution was lyophilised to obtain C. dots in powdered form free of residual unreacted species. The final concentration of the stock solution was maintained at 1 mg/mL. Schematic illustration of the steps involved in the formation of C. dots is shown in Scheme S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of PA and bilirubin
ce:section.ce:para: 500 µL of C. dots solution (1 mg/mL) was mixed with PBS buffer (1 mM, pH 7.4) to keep the system under constant pH. 30 µL of PA was added to the above solution from the stock of 0.1 mM strength, and the final volume of the solution was made to 3 mL. The reaction mixture was gently shaken and subsequently, the fluorescence measurement of the solution was carried out. The experiment was repeated with different concentrations of PA to confirm the sensitivity.

The stock solution of bilirubin (1 × 10−3 M) was prepared by taking 0.01 g of bilirubin in 25 mL of distilled water with 50 µL of 0.1 M NaOH (just to dissolve). 300 µL of bilirubin was then mixed with 1 mL of C. dots (1 mg/mL) and then made up to 3 mL with distilled water. All the interfering ions concentrations were also kept at 0.1 mM. No significant quenching of C. dots fluorescence was observed with the same amount of NaOH without bilirubin. Sample preparations and experiments were performed at room temperature. The excitation wavelength was maintained at 360 nm for both PA and bilirubin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of standard solutions for real sample assay
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of bilirubin in real sample, a standard calibration curve was plotted by taking 1 mL of C. dots solution from a stock of 1 mg/mL with different concentration range of bilirubin from stock solution of 1 mM and making the final volume to 3 mL with phosphate buffer of pH 7.4. After 5 min. incubation under room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360 nm excitation wavelength. A calibration curve was plotted by taking fluorescence intensity at 442 nm against concentration of bilirubin to determine the real sample bilirubin concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection of real sample and detection of bilirubin
ce:section.ce:para: For assay of bilirubin in human serum, the blood sample was collected from Sickle cell anemia patient with hyperbilirubinemia condition admitted at MAM’s Sane Guruji Hospital, Hadapsar, Pune with prior ethical committee approval. The obtained blood sample was centrifuged at a speed of 8000 rpm and the supernatant serum sample was collected and immediately stored at 4 °C for further analysis. The obtained serum sample was diluted 50 times before analysis. 1.5 mL of this sample was directly mixed with 1 mL of C. dots and made the final solution 3 mL with phosphate buffer. The fluorescence study was performed by exciting at 360 nm wavelength. For comparison, a standard procedure involving the diazo method was also followed to independently determine the bilirubin concentration in serum sample.

Urine mimic sample preparation was performed as per the literature without any modifications [30]. Requisite amount of CaCl2 (0.089 g), MgSO4 (0.100 g), NaHCO3 (0.034 g), Na2C2O4 (0.003 g), Na2SO4 (0.258 g), NaH2PO4 (0.100 g), Na2HPO4 (0.011 g), NaCl (0.634 g), KCl (0.450 g), NH4Cl (0.161 g), sodium citrate (0.297 g), urea (2.427 g) and uric acid (0.034 g), were added in 200 mL of triple distilled water. This solution was used as urine mimic with proper dilutions.

Thin layer chromatography of C. dots sample showed two distinct spots (blue and green coloured under UV light) in TLC plate. In order to separate the two fractions, silica column chromatography was employed. 0.02 g of the lyophilized C. dots was introduced to the silica column after dissolving in 20 mL of methanol. The column was packed with 20 g of silica. Ethyl acetate, ethanol and water in 7:3:1 ratio was used as the eluent. The flow rate was adjusted to 5 mL/min. Two fractions with blue and green colour fluorescence under UV light was separated and collected for further analysis (blue C. dots and green C. dots). These two fractions were then rotary evaporated to concentrate the fractions and lyophilized to get the solid fractions.

In the recent past, an enormous variety of natural substances and several multi step synthetic methods were employed for the synthesis of C. dots. However, in most of the synthetic methods, dopants or surface passivating agents were employed either to increase the fluorescence quantum yield or to use them for specific applications. For eg. N-acetyl-L-cysteine was added with rice to prepare C. dots with N-and S doping [31]. P-doped C. dots were obtained by treating sucrose with phosphoric acid [32]. Carbonisation and etching of hair fibre was performed using concentrated H2SO4 followed by pH adjustment with NaOH [33]. In the present study, synthesis of C. dots was carried out with the naturally available sunflower seeds without employing any additives or solvents. The protein content in the sunflower seeds resulted in the formation of N doped C. dots.

The morphology and structure of C. dots synthesized from sunflower seeds were inspected by various analytical techniques. TEM images provided a direct observation of the size and morphology of the C. dots (Fig. 1 A). The C. dots were found to be uniformly distributed with spherical shape and were mono dispersed. Size distribution of C. dots was found to be in the range of 2–6 nm with an average size of 3 nm. HRTEM reveals the magnified image of C. dots with less distinct lattice fringes indicating the amorphous nature of C. dots (Fig. 1B). Graphical representation of the particle distribution of C. dots is shown in Fig. 1C. The SEM images of C. dots (Fig. S1A & B) showed monodispersed nature of particles with near spherical morphology similar to the observation by HRTEM. The elemental composition of C. dots was obtained by EDS analysis which showed the presence of elements such as carbon, oxygen and nitrogen with percentage composition of C-64%, O-27% and N-9% respectively (Fig. S2A). The elemental mapping which shows the distribution of these three elements is shown in Fig. S2B (1–3).

To get a good insight into the surface functionalities of C. dots, infrared spectrum was taken (Fig. 2 ). The characteristic peak at 3202 cm−1 showed the presence of OH/NH groups. The peak at 2966 cm−1 corresponded to the CH stretching vibration. The peak at 1664 cm−1 represented the CO groups from the carboxylic moiety indicating the presence of carboxyl group which provided C. dots with good water solubility and made them suitable for various biological and environmental applications. The peaks at 1586 cm−1 and 1433 cm−1 showed the bending vibration of NH and stretching vibration of CN bonds. The amino groups on the surface of C. dots were confirmed by ninhydrin colorimetric assay. C. dots showed a deep blue colour with the addition of ninhydrin which confirmed the presence of primary amine groups on the C. dots surface. 1H & 13C NMR spectrum of C. dots also indicated the presence of rich surface functionalities (Fig. S3A & B).

The surface chemical composition and chemical state of C. dots were examined through XPS spectra analysis. The XPS analysis showed the presence of mainly three elements such as C, O and N in the C. dot structure. The full scan spectrum of C. dots showed three peaks at 283.9, 530.1 and 397.4 eV which corresponded to C 1s, O 1s and N 1s respectively as shown in the Fig. 3 A. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) exhibited three main peaks, the peak at 283. 9 eV corresponded to CC, the peak at 284.6 eV is assigned to CO and the peak at 286.5 eV found to be consistent with CN bonds. O 1s spectrum showed two peaks at 530.1 and 531.6 eV corresponded to CO and COH groups in the C. dots structure (Fig. 3C). Two peaks at 397.4 and 398.6 eV observed in high resolution N 1s spectrum corresponded to the pyridinic nitrogen and amine nitrogen, respectively (Fig. 3D). The surface components as well as the core structure details obtained in XPS analysis are in good agreement with the FTIR data.

The optical properties of synthesized C. dots were characterised by UV–Vis. and fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–Vis. spectrum of C. dots showed two separate absorption bands at 272 nm and 315 nm (Fig. 4 A). The peak at 272 nm corresponded to π-π* transition of CC or CN bonds in the aromatic sp2 domains and the latter aroused due to n-π* transitions of carbonyl or amine functionalities on the surface of C. dots structure. The fluorescence intensity measurements of C. dots were carried out with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 290 nm to 430 nm (Fig. 4B). It was observed in the study that the C. dots exhibited excitation dependant emission spectra in which the emission intensity increased with excitation wavelength with a red shift and reaches a maximum at 442 nm when excited at 360 nm. The optical performance of C. dots depends on the type of precursors used, synthetic approaches and various types of post treatment methods. Generally the formation of C. dots from proteins follows four steps like denaturation, dehydration, aromatization and carbonization [34]. Here, in this study, helianthinin, the most abundant protein in sunflower seeds undergoes denaturation and dehydration at high temperature leading to aromatic structures which eventually forms C. dots. (Scheme S2).

The three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum (known as excitation emission matrix fluorescence, EEMF) of C. dots is shown in Fig. S4A. This provides a clear picture of the spread of emission spectra at all probable excitation wavelengths, where the centre part provides excitation and emission wavelength maximum. The fluorescence Q.Y of C. dots was found to be 9.5%, calculated using quinine sulphate as standard (Fig. S4 B). This fluorescence Q.Y was comparatively higher than the fluorescence Q.Y of C. dots obtained from other natural sources [35]. A detailed study relating to the pH and ionic strength dependency of C. dots are given in the supporting information (Fig. S5A and B). The average fluorescence lifetime of the C. dots were found to be 2.72 ns and they showed excellent photostability (Fig. S6A and B).

The potential of employing the prepared C. dots for the determination of explosive nitro aromatic like PA was checked. A remarkable quenching of C. dots fluorescence was observed by the addition of PA (Fig. 5 A). A good fluorescent probe should show good sensitivity along with selectivity which enables them to be used effectively in the complex environment. To find out the selectivity of C. dots towards PA, fluorescence quenching experiments were carried out with other nitro aromatics. PA was able to quench 80% fluorescence of C. dots at a concentration of 0.1 mM of PA while the quenching ability of other interfering ions was less than 20%. Sensitive detection of PA using C. dots were examined by taking a concentration range from 1 µM to 0.1 µM (Fig. 5B) and 10 nM to 100 nM (Fig. S7). As the concentration was increased, the fluorescence intensity got reduced with a slight red shift from 442 nm to 452 nm when excited at 360 nm. A calibration plot was developed to find out the extent of dependence of fluorescence intensity over a range of PA concentration. A good linear correlation was observed by plotting a graph with F0/F against PA concentration in the range of 20–60 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99. Where, F0 and F denote the emission intensities at 442 nm in the absence and presence of PA respectively (Fig. 6 ). The limit of detection (LOD) of PA was found to be 3.86 nM (0.88 ppb) as calculated using 3σ rule. This proves the potency of C. dots as a selective and sensitive fluorescence probe for PA. Previously reported works of PA detection using C. dots has been compared with the present work in Table 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence quenching of C. dots by PA
ce:section.ce:para: Two mechanisms are mainly considered for understanding the quenching process, i.e. IFE and electron transfer interactions [37]. In our detailed investigation, it was observed that both these mechanisms are responsible for the effective quenching of fluorescence of C. dots by PA. The absorption spectrum of PA covers both the excitation and emission spectrum of C. dots, thus showing the possibility of IFE (Fig. 7 A). It was further verified by taking the absorption spectrum of C. dots-PA solution which shows an enhancement in the absorbance values of PA with no new absorption peak (Fig. S8). IFE exhibited by other interfering ions was found to be negligible as compared to PA as shown in the graph (Fig. 7B). Likewise, the second mechanism of electron transfer processes is also equally important in this system due to the presence of amino groups of C. dots. PA being highly electron deficient can interact effectively with the electron rich amino groups of C. dots resulting in the formation of exciplex complexes due to electron transfer. This type of interaction was confirmed by comparing the quenching trend of C. dot-PA system in water and ethanol (Fig. S9). The experiment was conducted by taking PA in the concentration range from 2 × 10−5 to 1.2 × 10−4 M. It was found that at lower concentration, a linear quenching of C. dots fluorescence with PA was observed for both in ethanol and water. However, at higher concentrations of PA, the system showed an enhancement in quenching in water as compared to ethanol. Water, being more polar, causes enhanced electrostatic interaction between C. dots and PA which in turn resulted in the quenching of fluorescence of C. dots. Hence, it can be concluded that the high fluorescence quenching of C. dots by PA could be due to the synergistic effect of both of these mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of C. dots as chemo sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Practical application of C. dots was examined by incorporating it in a suitable gel medium. An in-situ C. dots based chemo sensor was developed by making agarose hydrogel as a solid platform for C. dots. Agarose is a natural polymer extracted from sea weeds and found application as gel matrix for DNA and proteins in gel electrophoresis and many other gelation processes. It possesses high thermal, physical and chemical stability. Pores are produced due to gelation which becomes favourable for C. dots encapsulation and H-bonding interactions between NH2 and OH groups of C. dots with OH groups of agarose. Agarose (0.5 g) was added to 10 mL of C. dots solution (1 mg/mL) and heated near upto boiling to dissolve agarose completely into to the C. dots solution. This was transferred into suitable glassware for the desired shape and dried. These C. dots-agarose discs showed an intense blue colour under UV light and found to be physically stable showing the homogeneous distribution of C. dots into the polymer matrix (Fig. 8 ). The above disc was placed in the PA solution (100 μM) for about 1 min. It was observed that the colour of the disc was changed from light yellow to dark yellow under visible light and the bright blue fluorescence of the disc were quenched considerably under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and sensitive detection of bilirubin
ce:section.ce:para: Application of C. dots as biomolecule sensor is currently gaining momentum, while the detection of bilirubin using C. dots has not explored at length [42]. Here, we have used C. dots obtained from sunflower seeds for the sensing of bilirubin based on IFE. The absorption spectrum of bilirubin covers a range of wavelength from 332 nm to 506 nm which overlaps significantly with both excitation and emission spectra of C. dots (Fig. S10). The possibility of any kind of electron transfer interactions from fluorophore to analyte is ruled out, as bilirubin itself is electron rich molecule with lone pair of electrons on N-atoms in the pyrrole ring.

To find out the selectivity for bilirubin detection over other co-existing biomolecules and biologically important metal ions, a series of biomolecules, amino acids and metal ions were selected. The interfering biomolecules selected were creatinine, glucose, glutathione, fructose, urea and haemoglobin (Fig. S11A). The amino acids selected for the sensing included aspartic acid, arginine, glycine, lysine, cysteine, alanine, histidine, glutamic acid and proline (Fig. S11B). Biologically active metal ions were selected to find out the competency of C. dots towards bilirubin detection (Fig. S11C). All the possible interfering ions were kept at the concentration of 0.1 mM. It was found that there was a significant quenching of fluorescence of C. dots with bilirubin as compared to other biomolecules, amino acids and metal ions, thus proving its selectivity. This quenching was clearly visible under UV light also. Considering this vital finding of fluorescence quenching of C. dots with bilirubin, we evaluated the sensitivity of this system. The sensitivity of bilirubin was calculated by taking a series of concentrations of bilirubin from 1 to 10 μM. The quenching showed a concentration dependency where quenching of C. dots increased with increase in concentration of bilirubin (Fig. 9 A). This linear correlation tendency was explained using the equation F0/F = 1 + 56150.72X, where X denoted the concentration of bilirubin. The LOD of bilirubin using C. dots were obtained as 2.03 μM (0.118 mg/dL) (Fig. 9B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of bilirubin in real sample
ce:section.ce:para:
After a detailed study regarding the individual interaction of other biomolecules towards the C. dots fluorescence, it was found that the quenching efficiency of bilirubin was superior when compared to other biomolecules. To understand the practicality of our sensor system, the detection of bilirubin in human serum sample and urine sample was conducted.
(a)
Human serum sample with hyperbilirubinemia condition

To explore the practicability of our system for detection of bilirubin in real sample, blood samples were collected from hyperbilirubinemia patient. Those with hyperbilirubinemia condition are having bilirubin concentration level above 1.2 mg/dL. The C. dots were mixed with serum sample with proper dilution. A quenching of fluorescence intensity was observed when serum containing bilirubin was added to the C. dots. Calibration curve was plotted with the standard solution of bilirubin and using this calibration curve, concentration of bilirubin in the human serum sample was determined (Fig. 10 ). The obtained bilirubin concentration was found to be 7.01 ± 0.002 mg/dL (mean ± SD, n = 3), where SD is the standard deviation. To validate the reliability of C. dots for bilirubin detection, comparison was also made between the results obtained by the present method with that of the diazo method [43]. From the diazo method the obtained concentration of bilirubin was found to be 6.4 ± 0.3 mg/dL. This shows the possibility of C. dots system for real sample analysis of bilirubin.
(b)
Detection of bilirubin in fabricated urine sample

For this study, the urine mimic sample with C. dots was spiked with various concentrations of bilirubin. The urine sample dilution was performed in order to eliminate the interference from co-existing ions. 100 μL of prepared urine mimic was taken and diluted with 10 mL of water. 500 μL of this sample was mixed with 1 mL of C. dots followed by the addition of different concentrations of bilirubin (30–300 μL from the stock of 0.l mM) at optimized reaction conditions with PBS buffer of pH 7.4. It was observed that the diluted urine sample without bilirubin was not affected the fluorescence of C. dots. A calibration curve was plotted and the linear relationship was verified by two urine mimic samples with 3.5 and 9.5 μM bilirubin concentrations (Table 2 ). The recovered bilirubin concentrations were found to be 3.6 ± 0.2 μM (0.21 mg/dL) and 9.7 ± 0.3 μM (0.56 mg/dL) with % recovery of 102.85 and 103.15 respectively. Here we have used the concentration of bilirubin well below the hyperbilirubinemia levels of adults and newborn indicating that our nanoprobe can be effectively applied in both the cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fractions of C. dots obtained by column chromatography
ce:section.ce:para: Since the C. dots synthesized from natural sources contains multi components, the purification of C. dots can significantly fine tune its properties. The separation into various fractions provides a better understanding of the optical properties of C. dots and its effectiveness for various sensing applications [44]. Even though researchers tried to analyse the internal composition of C. dots, the fact that C. dots usually contain mixtures of several fractions makes this attempt more intricate. Therefore, purification and careful separation of the various fractions of C. dots is essential for structural analysis and for the isolation of different fractions of C. dots with different optical properties.

The column chromatographic separation was employed to separate and purify the different fluorescent components present in the C. dots as they showed two distinguishable spots on thin layer chromatography plate (TLC). Fractionation of the C. dots provided two separate fractions depending on their retention time in the chromatographic column. The fractions were coloured green (green C. dots) and blue (blue C. dots) when viewed in an UV cabinet. During the column separation, green C. dots were separated first followed by blue C. dots (Fig. S12). The absorption spectra of the obtained fractions were compared with the parent C. dots. It was found that both the fractions have π-π* and n-π* absorption bands as in the case of C. dots with a shift in the wavelength. Highly fluorescent blue C. dots showed two distinct absorption bands at 245 nm and 353 nm confirming the presence of fluorophores attached with the carbon core structure (Fig. 11 A). The peaks were not distinct for green C. dots as they are formed by increase in degree of surface oxidation. This is evident from the fluorescence spectra also, where presence of oxygen containing groups caused a fluorescence quenching of green C. dots. The blue C. dots exhibited a high fluorescence Q.Y of 14.9% (Fig. S13), whereas green C. dots showed a fluorescence Q.Y of 2% only. Interestingly, the fluorescence spectra of both the fractions showed excitation independent emission spectra which were entirely different from the fluorescence spectra of parent C. dots. This indicates the monodispersed and homogeneous nature of particles present in the separated fractions. λem for blue C. dots was observed at 425 nm for λex at 360 nm (Fig. 11B). Green C. dots showed λem at 498 nm for λex at 360 nm (Fig. 11C). The optical sensing of blue C. dots were further evaluated for PA and bilirubin detection based on two criteria 1) high fluorescence Q.Y of the blue fraction 2) The possibility of IFE for both analytes with the blue C. dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PA using blue C. dots
ce:section.ce:para: The blue C. dots obtained after the fractionation was used for the optical detection of PA. A concentration based quenching was observed in the concentration range from 1 nM to 10 nM of PA (Fig. 12 A). The S.V plot gave the LOD of 1.5 nM (0.343 ppb) using 3σ rule (Fig. 12B). It was observed that there was a substantial decrease in the LOD of PA in case of purified blue fraction of C. dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improved detection of bilirubin using C. dots
ce:section.ce:para: The bilirubin detection using C. dots was carried out in a concentration range of 1–10 µM (Fig. 13 A). When compared to the parent C. dots, the blue C. dots showed an improved sensing ability towards bilirubin and the LOD was found to be 0.27 μM (Fig. 13B).

In this study, a green synthetic approach based on hydrothermal method was adopted for the synthesis of C. dots from naturally available sunflower seeds. The obtained fluorescent C. dots were highly water dispersible and photo stable with a quantum yield of 9.5%. The IFE exhibited by PA and bilirubin towards the fluorescence of C. dots was successfully utilised for the detection of both. The fluorescence of C. dots was effectively quenched by PA with a limit of detection in the nanomolar range. A polymer sensing platform was fabricated for the easy and in-situ detection of PA. Bilirubin showed an effective fluorescence quenching with a LOD of 2.03 μM. Human serum sample and urine mimic sample were used as real sample for analysis and showed a good recovery. Column separated blue C. dots were found to show excellent quantum yield and excitation independent emission. Employing blue C. dots, for sensing PA and bilirubin enabled a higher sensitivity in detection compared to the parent C. dots. Thus, a green and facile nano sensor with good biological and environmental application which in future can be developed into active part of sensing device is proposed in this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumental Facility (SAIF) and Central Surface Analytical Facility of IIT Bombay for HRTEM and XPS facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.119354.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
